course_description: Economics focuses on choices between alternative ways of using resources and the consequences of these choices, which often have to be made in the face of problems associated with the availability of information and limitations to knowledge. These choices concern issues such as what to consume, what to produce, how to produce it, how to organize production between different economic entities, and what prices to charge. Choices taken by individuals need to be coordinated to avoid wasted opportunities, the more so the more that specialization is taking place at the level of the individual, firm or national economy. A major coordinating role is played by price mechanisms, i.e. adjustments in relative prices and the incentives provided by profits and losses. However, much coordination is achieved via the use of planning and institutions. Even with coordination assisted by these devices coordination failures can still occur, such as major slumps in employment and international trade. Economic theory can help to assess when these problems are likely and how they may be dealt with.

requirements.incompatible: ECON2010, or 2400 or 7003 or 7010 or 7011 or MEBS7103
requirements.assumed_background: No background in economics is assumed. However, if you want to do some reading before the course starts so as to get a feel for the economic way of thinking, then a good source for microeconomics would be Tim Hardford (2006) The Undercover Economist , Oxford, Oxford University Press. For macroeconomics, perhaps try Michael Stewart, Keynes and After (various editions), Harmondsworth, Penguin.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: To provide students with the economic principles underpinning much thinking in other management disciplines such as marketing and strategic management. To demonstrate why managers might benefit from a sound knowledge and ability to apply economic analysis and techniques. To help students move away from a simplified black and white way of thinking about economics, and move more towards seeing issues from different perspectives so as to make commitments using well argued reasoning. To develop skills in identifying which economic tools may be useful in particular situations and then applying these tools to analyse issues businesses face.

learning_outcomes.description: Employ economic concepts to critically appraise aspects of firms' strategic choices.
Demonstrate an ability to use economic concepts to conduct analysis rather than merely reporting on economic behaviour.
Demonstrate awareness of the relationship between economics and other management subjects such as marketing and finance.
Identify situations where economic theory will assist in arriving at well reasoned business decisions.
Employ the knowledge of a variety of economic concepts to deal with resource allocation problems.
Analyse the possible ramifications of events affecting a firms macroeconomic environment.
Evaluate the threats and opportunities a firm may face due to technological change and the globalization of the world economy.
Employ economic concepts to critically analyse strategic directions taken by organisations, as well as assess business related issues appearing in the daily press.

assessment_details.title: Individual Essay
assessment_details.task_description:
Major Assignment .

The focus of this course is to better understand economic concepts so as to then apply them in business situations. This, in part, will require interventions at times by managers and choices to be made in order to coordinate information and knowledge to avoid wasted opportunities. The course also highlights the importance of understanding the roles consumption, investment, and government actions play in taking business decisions, coordinating activities, and assessing strategic directions.

Economic theories and concepts used in this course have examined how and why businesses and markets work the way they do. With this knowledge, students have been given a tool to assist in making more informed, strategic, business decisions.

Using the perspectives outlined above, write an essay reflecting on a variety of economic concepts that impact your organization (where you are currently working, or have worked in the past). The essay must address the following points.

1. Provide a brief background of your organization.

2. Outline the key economic concepts you believe are relevant to your organization.

3. Critically analyse aspects of your organization that demonstrate your understanding of the key economic concepts you have identified. The analysis should reflect on:

i) Possible past successes or strengths, failures or weaknesses observed in your organization.

ii) Why successful and unsuccessful outcomes occurred.

iii) What options could be taken to reinforce any perceived/real strengths, or to rectify any weaknesses.

Note: Please be sure to use analysis, incorporating economics concepts. Dont just describe events and outcomes .

4. Based on your analysis, what recommendation(s) would you suggest your organization implements? Be sure these recommendations are practical with any implementation problems/issues discussed and addressed.

5. A conclusion reviewing the key elements and findings of the essay.

NOTE: The essay is to be between 2,500 and 3,000 words (12 point font, Times New Roman, double line spacing, 2.5 cm margins). Appropriate headings should be used in the essay that guides the reader.

assessment_details.title: Individual Newspaper Article Reviews
assessment_details.task_description:
Newspaper Article 1 (15%).

By referring to any Australian or International newspapers, find one article that relates to at least one microeconomic concept discussed in Lectures 1 to 6 (Monopoly Lecture) inclusively. This news article must be written in English and published after 1 January, 2010 . You may also refer to newspaper websites or other online newspaper sources to obtain your article. Your selected article must satisfy these minimum criteria in order to be able to pass this piece of assessment.

Using 500 to 750 words (with 12 point font, double line spacing, Times New Roman font, and 2.5cm margins), address each of the following criteria.

i) Describe the overall basis of the article in a few sentences.

ii) List the microeconomic concept(s) involved in the article.

iii) Critically analyse your article, making reference to the microeconomic concept(s) you have listed. Reflect on any strengths or weaknesses you perceive in the article. Assess how well you believe the article communicates an economic understanding to the reader.

iv) Using your analysis in iii), give an example of any real business (other than those mentioned in the article) that could be affected by the microeconomic concept(s) raised. Briefly justify why this would be so.

v) If you were the manager for the business you selected in iv), make at least one recommendation you would implement based on your analysis of the article.

vi) Conclude by stating what relevance you believe the concept(s) in the article have for a business manager.

vii) Photocopy (onto an A4 page) the article you have reviewed. Ensure the name of the newspaper, date published , and page number are on this photocopied article (hand written if need be). Attach this as the last page of your news article review submission.

Newspaper Article 2 (15%).

As was required for Newspaper Article 1 , refer to any Australian or International newspapers and find one article that relates to at least one microeconomic concept discussed in Lectures 1 to 6 (Monopoly Lecture) inclusively. This article must be written in English and published after 1 January, 2010 . You may also refer to newspaper websites or other online newspaper sources to obtain your article. Your selected article must satisfy these minimum criteria in order to be able to pass this piece of assessment.

Using 500 to 750 words (with 12 point font, double line spacing, Times New Roman font, and 2.5cm margins), cover points i) to vii) as was required for Newspaper Article Review 1.

(Note: Your first article may be submitted on 9 April at 9am in the lecture. It will be marked over the weekend of 10th April, and returned with feedback to the FRC on Monday 12th April by 12 noon. Students are encouraged to submit the first article on 9th April in order to benefit from any feedback for their second article).

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Case Analysis
assessment_details.task_description:
Group Presentation.

Students are required to form small groups of three to four students (group size will depend on actual enrollment numbers). The presentation is to be 20 to 25 minutes in length. The topic, date and time for each presentation will be allocated in the first tutorial.

Each group will be allocated, and required to focus on, one of the following companies listed on the Australian Stock Exchange: Chochlear (COH), JB- HiFi (JBH), Wesfarmers (WES), Papyrus (PPY), Devine (DVN), and McCarther Coal (MCC). Students are expected to access information from company websites, the ASX website, news articles, or other similarly available sources. Students should not contact or approach any company directly for information. The specific requirements of the group presentation are summarised in the Group Presentation Marking Guide. This guide is included with this course profile document (see attached link below). Students are expected to address each of the criteria in their presentation, so please refer to this Marking Guide carefully.

The specific requirements of the Group Presentation are:

1. Introduction and brief overview of your allocated company and its operations.

2. State the key economic theories and concepts your presentation will focus on. These should be the central theme of your presentation. COH, JBH, WES, and PPY should focus on microeconomic concepts. DVN and MCC are to focus on both micro and macro concepts, with the focus more toward macro impacts where possible.

3. Analyse your companys current performance and their chosen strategic direction. Highlight the specific choices the company has made. Assess the logic of these strategic choices (considering the environment, available resources, and existing market conditions). It should be clear by this stage as to why you selected your key economic theories. Demonstration of these economic theories and concepts should be clear through practical applications relevant to your company.

4. Fr om your analysis, identify current strategic issues the company is facing. Suggest possible options and choices managers could adopt to address these issues. Indicated what choice(s) you would recommend to the CEO and board of the company to address these issues. Focus on your key economic theories and concepts to support your recommendations.

After the presentation concludes, your group must then have one or two well thought out questions to promote class discussion and/or debate. The group (or nominated member) is required to lead a class discussion for an expected 10 15 minutes (or longer if the class is willing). The discussion should aim to expand the theoretical concepts explored in the presentation. To conclude this discussion, an assessment by the group should be made as to the practicalities and limitations of applying the selected economic theories to your allocated company.

Any other calculator brought into the examination room will be confiscated.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Introduction and Setting the Scene. (Lecture): Examine the linkages between economic theories and strategic management concepts. Introduce the notion of economic analysis as being one of various tools available to make strategic business decisions. Outline core economic concepts including the cost benefit principle, decision making pitfalls, opportunity cost, marginal analysis, and economic surplus. Chapter 1 and Chapter 2. Readings: Barney (1991), Hambrick and Frederickson (2001), Porter (2008), Whittington (2001).
Tutorial 1 (Tutorial)

What price do we charge? Part 1. (Lecture): Understanding supply and demand, using supply and demand models in business decision making, predicting likely market responses to different choices and strategies ensuring efficient uses of resources. Chapter 3 and Chapter 5 (p137). Readings: Peteraf (1993).
Tutorial 2 (Tutorial)

What price do we charge? Part 2. (Lecture): The concept of elasticity of demand and supply. Examine the application and implications of elasticity theory for business managers and pricing strategies. Chapter 4.
Tutorial 3 (Tutorial)

Economic Efficiency and Market Failure. (Lecture): Defining key terms such as producer and consumer surplus, maximizing the surplus, and dead weight loss. Assessing the impacts on free markets where governments intervene. Examine price ceilings, price floors, black-markets, impacts of subsidies, quotas, and import tariffs, and the implications for businesses. Chapter 5.
Tutorial 4 (Tutorial)

Profit Maximising using Microeconomics Theory. (Lecture): Assess short run and long run productions costs of a firm, Total Cost, Average Cost, Marginal Cost. Introduce the theory of Perfect Competition as a basis for analyzing more realistic market structures. Examine a perfectly competitive firms profit maximizing output, loss minimizing and shutdown situations. Chapter 6 and Chapter 7. Readings: Richardson (1999)
Tutorial 5 (Tutorial)

Monopolies, Cartels and the ACCC. (Lecture): How monopolies emerge, how a monopoly maximizes profit, real world applications of the theory, the implications to society from both economic and business perspectives, the role of the ACCC and the economic theory behind their decisions, cartel behavior and some recent examples. Chapter 8. Readings: Davies and Davies (1984)
Tutorial 6 (Tutorial)

Monopolistic and Oligopoly Markets. (Lecture): Monopolistic firms, their business strategies and characteristics, profit maximising criteria, comparisons to other market structures and implication for strategic decision making. Examine oligopoly market characteristics and introduce Game Theory. Chapter 9. Readings: Markides and Williamson (1994), Peteraf (1993)
Guest Lecture - Microeconomics (Guest Lecture)

Price Discrimination and Asymmetric Information. (Lecture): The differing forms of price discrimination and considering how and when they are used. First, second and third degree price discrimination, Inter-temporal Pricing, Peak Load Pricing, Two Part Tariff, and the linkages to market structures, resources, and capabilities. Implications of asymmetric information and imperfect information in decision making. Lemons model, Principal-Agent problem, Adverse Selection, Moral Hazard. Chapter 8 (from p250) and Chapter 11 (from p347).
Group Presentations (two groups)

Introduction to Macro Economics. (Lecture): Introduction to Macro Economics. Key concepts including the Circular Flow of Funds Model for GDP, economic growth and the Solow Growth Model (plus endogenous growth ideas), understanding inflation and the CPI, and models to understand aspects of unemployment. Chapter 12 and Chapter 14. Readings: Odlyzko (2004), Radford (1945), Solow (1994)
Tutorial 7 (Tutorial): Topics from L7, L8, L9.

Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply (AD-AS). (Lecture): Establish linkages between various economic variables using the AD-AS Model. Review reasons for shifts in aggregate demand and supply, and using the AD-AS model consider implications of various macroeconomic events. Show how the model is applied to predict real world possible outcomes from specific policy actions. Chapter 13.
Group Presentations (two groups)

Monetary Policy (by Karen Hooper). (Lecture): Outlining the objectives and implementation of monetary policy in Australia and the role of the Reserve Bank of Australia. An overview of the Bank's inflation targeting framework and current economic conditions. Chapter 15 and Chapter 16.
Group Presentation (one group)

Fiscal Policy. (Lecture): Defining and applying Fiscal Policy. Government deficits and surplus, levels of debt, crowding out private investment. Understanding the various policy measures governments take to stabilise economies during periods of boom and bust. Applying theoretical models to help analyse fiscal policy decisions. Adopt the models to provide a framework to appreciate the ongoing political debates about an economy. Examine how to apply the models to make more informed strategic business decisions. Chapter 17.
Group Presentation (one group)
Overview of Course Concepts (Review): Review course concepts and student essay findings. General concluding comments on the applicability of course theories for business managers.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

Definition of Plagiarism

Plagiarism is the act of passing off as your own work another persons writing, words, or ideas. You must make it clear which ideas you have obtained from someone else. Superficial and minor changes do not disguise your use of the words of someone else. You commit plagiarism if you do not acknowledge the source of a direct quote, or a specific piece of writing that you have paraphrased, or even if you describe an idea or concept that you have heard or read somewhere without a reference or acknowledgement.

Examples of plagiarism as given in the Universitys Academic Integrity and Plagiarism Policy are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Offering an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

1. The first is that when another writers work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as ones own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the Universitys Academic Integrity and Plagiarism Policy. The penalty for plagiarism under the Student Discipline and Misconduct Statute can range from a fine not exceeding 1 penalty point to exclusion from the University.

2.The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those follow-up papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

3.Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the School of Economics Assignment Presentation Guide.

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Statute No. 4 (Student Discipline and Misconduct) 1999 to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a fine not exceeding 1 penalty unit;

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on their student record.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Other
School of Economics
Guidelines

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers a range of economic theories and concepts useful in the business environment. With no prior knowledge of economics, it aims to provide students with a solid grounding in fundamental economic theories relevant for business managers. In the first lecture, the linkage between economic theories and strategic business decisions is explored. This linkage then forms an underlying theme throughout the course. This theme is developed not only in lectures, but also through student activities including reviewing current newspaper articles, in-class case presentations, and writing a reflective essay on their current work organization. These activities are all aimed at developing an understanding of economic theories and how they can strategically and practically be applied in business environments. The early lectures introduce fundamental terminology and concepts in economics, with an emphasis on price setting, profit maximization, costs, and differing market structures. Later, problems in allocating available resources with limited information and knowledge is discussed. An appreciation of linkages between economic concepts and business disciplines such as marketing, finance, and human resource management should also become evident through the course. Having assessed organizations from a microeconomic perspective, the course then concludes by considering how organizations fit into the bigger macroeconomic picture. In the last lectures, the course focus is on GDP, growth, inflation, unemployment, aggregate demand and supply, monetary and fiscal policy, and their relevance to business managers and decision making.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

0-19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20-44%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

A common cause of students getting this grade on an answer, aside from having done little work or achieved a poor understanding, is a failure to answer the question that was set (for example, if the question says 'explore the relationships between the following concepts', you must explore the relationships between them, not define or describe them), or a failure to attempt all parts of the question. So, read the wording very carefully. 45-49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic requirement of being able to think like an economist is demonstrating that you consider the relative costs and benefits of alternative possible choices and the implications of differences in the incentives they offer to decision makers. If you fail to do this, you may have a hard time getting a 4, let alone a better grade, even if you look at the pros and cons of a particular choice from the standpoint of relevant economic theory. 50-64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Note here the phrase 'ability to apply': if your answers primarily report on facts that you have gathered or merely summarize the work of others you will be failing to show what you can do with economic concepts as tools of analysis and you will not score more than a 4. For a 5 of beyond, you must show that you can think like an economist rather than merely displaying abilities in terms of researching facts or what some economists think. 65-74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

To get beyond a 5, it is helpful to be able to demonstrate that you know what is predicted by relevant economic theory in the context in question and consider how well the predictions match up with facts. 75-84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Integrative thinking that relates concepts from different parts of the course will be highly regarded as will the application of ideas/arguments from reading beyond the prescribed text or lecture notes. 85-100%

Other Requirements & Comments:
late_submission: A late penalty may be applied (up to 1% from your final score per day) unless an extension has been granted previously.
other_assessment_information:
ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

Unless otherwise specified, all assignments are to be submitted as specified in section 5.5. Each assignment must be accompanied by the appropriate, fully completed Assignment Cover Sheet, available from www.bel.uq.edu.au . Students must sign the cover sheet, acknowledging the plagiarism Statute of the University and must always keep a copy of their work. Assignments must not be faxed or emailed to the Course Coordinator without prior arrangement. All assignments will be signed and dated when received by the Course Coordinator. If the assignment is not able to be delivered at the scheduled lecture, it should be place in the assignment slot of Faculty Resource Centre (FRC) on Level 1 of the Colin Clark Building (#39) where it will be stamped and dated when received. Please send an email to Course Coordinator, within 24 hours, if an assignment is delivered to the FRC. The group presentation should be accompanied with a copy of any slides used in the presentation with ONE nominated representatives details at the top of the Assignment Cover Sheet, with details of other group members listed on the lower section. Students should keep a list of their group members' full names and contact details. Note that students must produce their student ID card (or drivers license) to collect marked assignments.

SPECIAL EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a special examination at a later time.

Information about special examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/special-examinations Please read this information very carefully.

All applications for special exams must be submitted within (5) days of the original date of the examination.

End of Semester Examination (SCHOOL EXAM) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at : http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/supplementary-examinations-2901 Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Special & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (22-24 July).
